summary: The rate of change measures how fast a function's output changes. The average rate of change over an interval is the slope of the secant line between two points; the instantaneous rate of change at a single point is the slope of the tangent line — the derivative evaluated there.
formulas: average rate of change on [a, b]:  (f(b) − f(a)) / (b − a)
instantaneous rate of change at a:  f '(a)
method: For an average rate, evaluate f at both endpoints a and b.
Divide the change in output, f(b) − f(a), by the change in input, b − a.
For an instantaneous rate, differentiate to get f '(x).
Substitute the point a into f '(x).

example.title: Average vs instantaneous rate
example.find: the average rate on [1, 3] and the instantaneous rate at x = 2
example.answer: Average rate = 4; instantaneous rate at x = 2 is 4
